data.title: XPO Logistics: A High-Quality Business That Is Fully Priced
data.description:
In September 2025, I initiated coverage for XPO Logistics (XPO) with a hold rating. As one of the largest providers of less-than-truckload freight transportation services, the company stood out with a disciplined approach towards capacity expansion, favoring unit revenue strength in a challenging freight market. However, the upside I saw was limited to 8%. The stock, however, has returned 15.5% since the publication of my first report, and for that reason I am revisiting the name with an updated price assessment.

XPO Logistics Navigates A Tough Market Environment

The freight and logistics market is a huge market. According to Mordor Intelligence, the total addressable market was $6.4 trillion in 2025 with the market expected to grow 5% annually to $8.1 trillion by 2030. Seemingly, that offers an attractive, stable growth opportunity. However, the size of the market also attracts many players that want a piece of the pie. The result is that the market is highly fragmented and extremely competitive, which can lead to supply-demand imbalances amplified by temporary macroeconomic weaknesses.

For many freight and logistics companies, the challenges started after travel restrictions eased during the pandemic period. It led to consumers changing spending habits and more freight capacity coming online. Freight and logistics companies had taken all opportunities to grow capacity during the lockdowns as supply was limited and demand was high. The end of lockdowns reversed that. Shortly after, the market was also hit by inflation and high-energy prices, putting pressure on the cost of services and demand once again. The trade war did not help either, and as a result, the freight and logistics market is going through a multi-year reset on the supply-demand mechanism.

Opportunities to cut costs include capacity cuts, thereby improving unit revenues, but other measures include in-sourcing of third-party operations. Cash can be preserved through a reduction in capital expenditures. XPO has the advantage that it can capitalize on a strong reputation.

Wall Street Expects Stable Revenues And Lower Earnings

That the market remains in a challenging position can be seen in the expectations for the fourth quarter. Revenue is expected to be between $1.91-$2.01 billion, with a consensus of $1.95 billion marking 1.7% growth. Over the past six months, analysts have revised revenue expectations downward by 1% with six analysts revising estimates up and eight analysts revising estimates down.

On earnings per share, we see that analysts expect $0.71-$0.82 with a consensus EPS estimate of $0.77, marking a 14% decline with all analysts revising estimates downward over the past three months. So, we see that while revenues are expected to hold rather well, cost remains an issue, and that provides somewhat of an indication that there still is so much capacity in the market that passing on higher costs or higher unit costs to customers is not possible.

XPO Stock Is Fairly Valued At Current Prices

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

XPO Logistics has exceeded my price target. However, at current prices, we see no strong upside for the stock. The stock is trading 5% above the implied price target, which I believe represents a fair valuation. EBITDA estimates have declined by 0.8%, but free cash flow estimates have increased by 5.5% driven by stronger cash conversion, which I believe reflects working capital management and cash collections. So, while the stock price did increase, at current price levels we see no upside but also no substantial downside. As a result, I am reiterating my hold rating.

Margins are expected to be flat for 2025 and tick up in the years after to around 17.5%. One of the drivers of the margins is an outflow of drivers expected in the US driven by stricter language requirements. This may tighten supply and drive up unit prices. Free cash flow is expected to continue growing as capital expenditures have largely been tapered. The company has a $750 million repurchase program, which I believe will ramp up in the years ahead. While the company returns value to shareholders, we will see the cash balance increase and net debt coming down. My current expectation is that the company will move its net-debt leverage from the upper limit of 3x to the lower limit of 2x. Anything between 2x and 3x is what I consider healthy.

Conclusion: The Entry Point Is Lacking For XPO

If you are happy with the entry point around fair value, then the current stock prices do provide that, as the stock is just 5% overvalued. However, I believe that provides little margin of safety, and that means that XPO currently does not offer an attractive entry point. The company does trade at a premium to peers and I believe its scale and premium that the company can charge due to its service reputation justify that. However, we note that all of that is already fully priced in. So, the premium service, premium EV/EBITDA, and strong balance sheet are already fully valued and provide no support for a buy rating.

data.title: Knife River Corporation: Risk/Reward Not So Attractive; Paying Attention To Next Results
data.description:
Investment Summary

My recommendation for Knife River Corporation (KNF) is a hold rating. I believe KNF has the right strategy in place and a strong moat. But near-term risks around weather and labor availability make me worried about how Q4 will play out. The scale gap vs. larger peers also limits multiple expansions. As of the time of writing, I think the risk-reward setup is not that attractive.

Business Overview

KNF is a spinoff from MDU Resources and is now a vertically integrated provider of construction materials and contracting services. KNF focuses on mid-sized, high-growth markets across the Mountain, West, and Central US regions. The strategy adopted is straightforward. They would enter a market, secure the aggregate reserves (aggregates are granular materials like sand, gravel, and crushed stone, sourced naturally or recycled, that form the bulk of composite materials like concrete and asphalt), and gun for the number-one position so that it is difficult for competitors to displace them. By doing so, KNF has amassed a large base of reserves (~1.2 billion tons of aggregate reserves), which can be fed to their own downstream production of ready-mix concrete and asphalt, which supports their contracting services division. This structure allows KNF to capture margin across the value chain.

Competitive Moat

KNF’s moat really stems from which part of the market it targets. As said above, KNF focuses on mid-sized high-growth markets, and because of that, competition is much less intense, with the potential for KNF to become the local leader. While the absolute deal size may be smaller relative to large markets, the volume of deals KNF can win is much higher because in these markets, competition tends to be sub-scale players (local players) that are often undercapitalized. What this means is there is a gap in the market where only a larger player is able to execute large, complex public infrastructure jobs.

When it comes to large, complex public projects, there are really two barriers to entry that effectively disqualify smaller players from major public projects. The first is financial capacity (which is sort of regulated), and the other is supply chain disadvantage. The reality in this industry is that public entities like the State Departments of Transportation don't just hire anyone to build a highway. They want a vendor that is able to put up a surety bond (basically a financial guarantee that the contractor has the funds to finish the job even if they go bust). A local player may have the funds to take on a deal to pave a driveway or a parking lot, but it is unlikely they have millions of dollars in the bank to insure a $500 million highway interchange build (for example).

Having a scale advantage vs. these smaller local players puts KNF in a significantly better position to control end pricing. Unlike local players that have to buy their rock or asphalt from a larger player (sometimes even from KNF) to do the job, KNF often owns the quarry, the asphalt plant, and the paving crew. This translates into structural cost savings, as KNF doesn’t need to pay a markup on materials. Worst of all, because KNF owns the supply chain, it doesn’t need to worry about raw material supply tightness (which could literally push smaller players out of any job since they don’t have the materials needed).

Therefore, when the job is large and complex and carries high financial risk, the customer is likely to choose the bigger player in these markets.

The other moat that KNF has accumulated over time. As of the latest filing, KNF controls 1.2 billion tons of aggregate reserves, which I see as a massive edge, as aggregates are finite, and for new supply to come online, a new quarry needs to come online, for which permits have become increasingly hard to secure. Things like environmental reviews, zoning restrictions, and opposition from the local community can delay projects for a very long time. In that sense, existing reserve functions can be seen as a literal barrier to entry. Say that a large project comes for bidding in a market that doesn’t have a quarry; local players simply cannot compete on price vs. KNF because they would need to purchase aggregates from a 3P (which includes a markup) vs. KNF, which already has it on their balance sheet.

What To Watch Out For In Q4 Earnings

KNF is going to report its Q4 2025 earnings in mid-February, and I think it is not going to be a clean positive quarter. Consensus is optimistic, calling for ~11% y/y revenue growth and ~33% y/y EBITDA growth, though that reflects the Strata acquisition and the execution of the cost savings program. However, I am taking a more conservative stance for this Q4 because of KNF's heavy Pacific Northwest exposure. Last month (December), the US saw severe atmospheric river activity across Washington and Oregon, which could impair KNF’s ability to deploy labor to continue with their projects. This could mean revenue growth getting pushed back to another quarter, resulting in a miss versus consensus.

I would pay a lot of attention to how management frames the weather impact (if any). If the weather impact is just a mere push out of work to be done and not lost, the market should not react negatively (since it is just a timing issue). But if otherwise, that could weigh on the stock sentiment.

Valuation

The two main peers for KNF are Vulcan Materials (VMC) and Martin Marietta Materials (MLM). These two players are much larger than KNF (revenue size >2x KNF’s), and I think that explains why KNF has historically traded at a discount (right now it's 23x forward earnings vs. peers at ~29-30x). As I said, scale matters in this industry, so VMC and MLM come aggressively and compete against KNF, but KNF cannot do the same against them. Hence, I think there is a ceiling to how much KNF’s valuation multiple can go up from here. For that to happen, KNF needs to either show significantly higher growth (it is expected to grow revenue at mid-single-digits vs. peers at high-single-digits), so much that it outweighs the scale differences. Until we see signs of that, I am expecting multiples to stay range-bound.

Risk

One risk I would add that makes me less bullish in the near term is labor constraint. As much as KNF has the reserves and is vertically integrated, the bottleneck is having enough employees to execute the job. And the problem is the industry is dealing with a persistent shortage of skilled workers. As such, although KNF has a huge backlog, they may not be translated into revenue or earnings, or even if they do, it may be slower than expected.

Conclusion

KNF has a strong moat; however, near-term execution risk, labor constraints, and a valuation ceiling due to its relative scale make me less bullish for now. I believe the business will do well over the long term, but I'd like to see signs of growth acceleration and also how KNF performs in Q4 before I can turn bullish.

data.title: Rates Spark: Steeper Curves Still Path Of Least Resistance
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

Recovering risk sentiment helps euro rates nudge back up again

Market sentiment is recovering, and euro rates, in particular, are taking this opportunity to rise higher. At Davos, we saw Trump refraining from escalating the Greenland tensions, further helping to improve sentiment. The 10Y swap rate is back to 2.9%, but still below the earlier high of 2.96%. Meanwhile, the growth outlook in the eurozone is still on an improving trajectory, which, together with high supply, helps build the case for higher rates in 2026.

A natural candidate to capture the improved risk sentiment and high issuance is a further steepening of the 5s10s curve this year. The spread between 10Y and 5Y captures the building of a term premium, the additional yield investors demand for holding longer maturities. Both quantitative tightening and increased government issuance should add to this term premium. In Europe, we likely won’t see sharp moves like we’ve seen in Japan this week, but the overall direction should also be for steeper curves.

EUR curve can still steepen further on supply pressure and improving growth

Thursday’s events and market view

The main releases of the day will come from the US with the Personal Income and Spending reports and the PCE price index, the Fed’s preferred inflation measure. A monthly core reading of 0.2%, as the consensus expects, would be seen as benign. We will also get the weekly jobless claims figures and the final 3Q GDP figure. In the eurozone, the ECB will release the minutes of the December meeting.

Primary markets will see France auctioning short- to medium-maturity bonds as well as inflation-linked bonds for an overall volume of more than €15bn. The US Treasury will sell US$21bn in new 10y TIPS.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Red Cat: Geopolitical Tailwinds To Boost This Defense Stock
data.description:
I had written about Red Cat (RCAT) in the past, and in my view, the company is well-positioned to take advantage of the current geopolitical tensions and the change in the way the future of warfare will be conducted. While the latest quarter results are uneventful, there have been some key developments that continue to fuel my bullish bias.

Quarter Results

Looking at the company’s most recent quarterly results shows that the company has grown its revenues 646% year over year and 200% over the prior quarter. These results have beaten consensus expectations and are a great headline number. However, comparing the last nine months ending in September shows that revenue growth is minimal and that the company has stayed flat for the year so far. In the 9 months of 2025 ending in September, the company has reported $14.5 million in revenue compared to the $14.3 million the same time last year.

This basically implies that Q3 2025 has basically made up the majority of 2025 revenues. This indicates to me a sort of lumpiness in the company’s revenues where it does well in certain quarters. In other words, Red Cat has not yet achieved steady order flow for its products. This is something I would want to look for in 2026 and beyond.

Another minor concern is the company’s reported gross margins are on the low side. For the latest quarter, Red Cat is reporting a positive gross profit of $637,502 with a 6.6% margin, which is better than the loss it reported at the same time last year. However, the last nine months ended September 30, 2025; the company’s gross profit is $162,146 with a 1% margin, which is less than the gross profit of $997,045 (7% margin) for the same period in 2024. Manufacturing companies need a certain level of economies of scale to hit decent gross margins. Red Cat’s current gross margins aren’t great, but, given that the company is still in its growth phase, are somewhat acceptable.

The most important part, though, of the company’s financials is its balance sheet and cash position. In Q3 2025, the company ended with a net loss of -$16 million, which translates to a loss of -$52 million for the nine months ending September 2025 compared to the net loss of -$12 million and -$26 million during the same time last year. A portion of this loss is driven by the increase in R&D spending in order to fund its latest innovations, which are discussed below. In the quarter, R&D investment hit $6 million, a 66% quarterly increase and nearly 3x the $1.9 million reported at the same time last year. Nine months in, R&D spending hit $12.9 million compared to the $4.8 million in 2024.

Total liabilities have also ballooned year-over-year as they increased from $5,484,714 in 2024 to $32.7 million at the end of this quarter. Luckily, the company is sitting on a decent amount of cash to fund its future growth at $206.4 million at the end of the quarter. Overall, I am not too concerned about the company’s liquidity and debt position, as it has, in my view, enough runway to execute on its goals. Overall, an uneventful quarter financially for Red Cat, but the real developments for the company are on the geopolitical front as well as its continued leaps in technology improvements.

Tensions between nations are a tailwind for Red Cat

The geopolitical environment on the war/defense front should prove very fruitful for the company’s products. It is my view that the future of warfare will not be fought with tanks or conventional weaponry but rather with drones and superior intelligence. Take, for example, the recent capture of Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro by US special forces. Such a daring operation was only made possible by high-tech surveillance and superior intelligence.

According to a BBC article, US spies had been monitoring Maduro’s every move for months, where he ate, where he slept, and every little move. The operation dubbed "Operation Absolute Resolve" was the result of months of meticulous planning and rehearsals by elite US troops and was unparalleled in terms of scale and precision. It is known that stealth and intelligence-gathering drones were a key part of the operations, as they provided the US military with the necessary surveillance. These key assets were linked with satellites that bounced data from assets on the ground to Washington, DC, and forward command bases. This is the type of lethal capability that Red Cat seeks to enhance for US combat troops as it enhances its drones.

But just to be clear, though, Red Cat did not play a role in this important military activity as far as we know. The capabilities that we can see using AI and drones are still aspirational and not yet fully operational. However, even this early, we can start to see the pieces come together.

For example, the company has successfully tested its integration of Palantir’s (PLTR) Visual Navigation (“VNav”) software on Red Cat’s state-of-the-art Black Widow drone. This test shows that the Black Widow can navigate in areas without GPS access using Palantir’s software. Palantir engineers reported a mean positional error of roughly 7 meters over a 2.7 kilometer route, indicating good performance in real-life conditions. No additional hardware is needed, as VNav runs entirely on the Black Widow’s existing onboard sensors. According to Red Cat CEO Jeff Thompson,

“This is a breakthrough moment not just for Red Cat, but for the tactical needs of the Department of War,…Every battlefield is a GPS-denied environment, and this successful test shows that Red Cat and Palantir are delivering a software-driven solution the Army can rely on. It requires no new hardware, is ready to deploy today, and gives warfighters the edge in contested environments. It also signals our evolution into a full-stack defense technology platform, with expected margin expansion and strong revenue potential in 2026.”

The company also announced a strategic collaboration with Safe Pro Group Inc. (SPAI), a leading provider of AI-driven computer vision. This technology will be embedded in Red Cat’s Black Widow system in order for it to rapidly identify and locate in real-time explosive threats such as landmines and unexploded ordinances. Powering Safe Pro’s technology is a massive landmine data set of over 1.88 million drone images analyzed to date, and 34,200+ threats identified across 8,119 hectares (20,062 acres) in Ukraine.

I like the fact that Red Cat, is able to partner with key technology providers in order to boost the capabilities of its drones. In some ways, I am looking at the company as the sort of “hardware” platform that AI/intelligence companies can sort of latch onto. The more partnerships Red Cat is able to secure, the greater the overall capabilities of its systems become. There have been a few key conflicts in recent memory that show that drones and intelligence will be the future of war. The latest incident in Venezuela shows that this is the case and that there really isn’t the political appetite for a “conventional” style of warfare. All this is good for Red Cat, which I believe could be a key player in this space.

2026 Could be a Breakout Year for Red Cat

Late last year, the company was able to obtain two important certifications. The 1st is being added to the NATO Support and Procurement Agency (NSPA) catalogue. This would allow NATO members and partners to purchase Red Cat’s Black Widow drones. The company also recently announced that its mass market FANG FPV drone system has been officially added to the Department of War’s Blue UAS Cleared List. This means that the company’s FANG system is able to meet the strict criteria of the Pentagon for cybersecurity, supply chain integrity, and operational trustworthiness. Thus, is now eligible for streamlined purchases by the Department of War and other federal agencies without cumbersome additional approvals or sourcing waivers.

This makes it easier now for federal departments to buy the company’s systems, thus hopefully resulting in a spike in sales and usage. Management has recently issued preliminary guidance for the fourth quarter and full year 2025 of $24 million to $26.5 million and full-year revenue of $38 million to $41 million.

Key Risks and Conclusion

At a market cap of $1.63 billion, this means that the company is trading at a price-to-sales multiple of 39.8x 2025 sales. I do believe, though, that the company is still in the beginning stages of accelerating its revenue. In my view, management is doing all the necessary steps from a technology perspective. Furthermore, looking at the company’s most recent financials, Red Cat has enough runway in terms of leverage and available cash in order to accomplish its goals.

In terms of cash burn, in the last nine months, Red Cat used $52.6 million of cash in its operations. This is a negative cash burn situation as the company is not yet making any money from its operations. The company currently has $206.4 million in cash in its balance sheets. Annualizing the cash loss from operations is about $70.1 million in cash burn annually, translating to about 2.94x. Note, though, that the company only has $350,000 worth of debt, indicating that it can still leverage its balance sheet to get additional runway (assuming, of course, that there are lenders willing to extend credit).

Like every tech stock in the beginning stages, this is still a bet on the future. The key risk is going to be on execution. So far, things are looking good, but will the company be able to properly scale its US-based manufacturing to meet the demand and make sure that the produced hardware is up to a certain standard? Furthermore, as seen when looking at the financials, gross margins aren’t particularly high. Since the company is primarily US-based, will it be able to hit profitability in its gross margins when it scales? Any bad news with regard to execution, such as a delay or loss of a key customer, will hit RCAT stock particularly hard, given its already high valuation.

So while it is, in my view, the tailwinds are in Red Cat’s favor, but then it is up to management to seize the moment. I continue to like what I see, though, and maintain my buy rating for the stock.

data.title: LyondellBasell FQ4: How I Diagnose Dividend Cuts
data.description:
LyondellBasell FQ4 Earnings: Dividend Coverage Brought Into Question

My last article on LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (NYSE:LYB) was published on Dec 10, 2025, under a title of "The LyondellBasell Absurdity: Yield Exceeds FY2 P/E." The article rated the stock as a buy, and other basic information is provided in the screenshot below. Since then, there have been a few notable developments surrounding LBY, especially surrounding the safety of its dividend coverage. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the two changes on my radar and explore their implications for its dividend coverage. The first change involves its upcoming FQ4 earnings report (ER) and latest EPS revisions, and the second one involves changes in the petrochemical market. After examining these changes, I do see pressure on its dividend coverage (especially in EPS terms) to remain in the next 2 years or so. However, I also want to caution investors about the limitations of accounting EPS. They can - and often do - paint a misleading picture. In the case of LYB, a holistic examination of its financials shows me that the EPS coverage ratio exaggerated the dividend pressure significantly.

With the above overview, let's take a closer look at its upcoming FQ4 ER scheduled on Jan 20, 2025, as shown in the chart below. Based on the latest EPS revisions, you can also see why LyondellBasell's dividend sustainability has been brought into question. The company's EPS projection for the upcoming quarter is around $0.27 currently, reflecting a sharp consequential decline from the $1.01 adjusted EPS reported in the September quarter. This downturn further pressures the already stretched dividend coverage ratio. The current quarterly dividend of $1.37 per share far exceeds the EPS in the last quarter (by more than 35%) and even more so for the next quarter's projected EPS.

LYB Stock: EPS Dividend Coverage Could Remain Pressured

Looking beyond the immediate term, LyondellBasell is likely to keep facing coverage pressure - at least in EPS terms - for the years to come. As just mentioned, the EPS coverage ratio for 2025 is likely to be far above 100% (with a projected EPS of $2.30 and an annualized payout of $5.48 per share at the current rate). A strong EPS recovery is forecasted starting in 2026, with EPS expected to grow by 45%, followed by similarly strong YOY growth rates. However, even at these rates, the EPS payout ratio will not drop below 100% until about 3~4 years later, as seen.

However, readers following our writings must know the limitations of accounting EPS. In our experience, recognizing these limitations is the starting point to tell value apart from value traps, especially for highly cyclical businesses like LYB. To start, we suggest investors at least check out the cash flow, as accounting income can distort its true financials due to extraordinary one-time items. For example, even though the company's accounting income is deeply negative as of TTM (at -$1,208 million as seen in its cash flow statement below), its operating cash flow is more than $2,670 million. The discrepancy is largely caused by accounting items related to asset write-down and reconstruction cost. In terms of organic cash flow, the company actually generated enough cash from its operations to cover its dividend payouts as of TTM ($2,670 million of cash flow vs. $1,758 million of payouts).

LYB Stock: But Dividend Cushion Ratio Remains Healthy

Moreover, simple payout ratios - either in terms of EPS or cash flow - completely ignored other important financial assets and obligations of the target stock. These factors include debt, payables, inventory, cash, receivables, etc. It is self-evident that two companies with the same EPS and/or cash payout ratios can have drastically different dividend safety outlooks if they differ in these aspects. If you subscribe to this notion too, then you would see why we like the dividend cushion ratio ("DCR"). This DCR concept is detailed in Brian M. Nelson's book, Value Trap. The gist is quoted below to ease reference.

The DCR is obtained by A) summing the existing net cash a company has on its balance sheet plus its expected future free cash flows over the next five years, and B) dividing that sum by future expected cash dividends (including expected growth in them, where applicable) over the same time period. If the ratio is significantly above 1, the company generally has sufficient financial capacity to pay out its expected future dividends. The higher the ratio, the better, all else equal.

In a nutshell, the DCR factors in a company's finances holistically in the assessment of its payout coverage. To wit, my estimate of LYB's DCR is shown below. These estimates were made following Nelson's above steps with one deviation. In the 1st step, Nelson's method subtracts all the liabilities to obtain the net cash position. But for a business with LYB status, I don't see any realistic chance that requires the company to repay all its debt at once. The more realistic scenario in my mind is that LYB can renew large portions of its debts in the mid- or long-term. It just needs to service the interest costs and cover its current debt obligations. With these considerations, I only subtracted the current liabilities and also the interest expenses in my analysis.

With this caveat, my estimate for LYB's DCR in 2025 is around 2.62x, as shown. The bad news is that it represents a deterioration, especially when compared to the DRC in 2023 and also 2024. Also, my projected DRC also falls below the historical average of 2.97x between the six years of 2019 and 2024. But the good news is that the projected DCR of 2.72x is still well above the safety threshold of 1x.

Valuation, Other Risks, and Final Thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks are worth mentioning, both in the positive and negative directions. The near- and mid-term outlook for some of LYB's key petrochemicals, in particular polyethylene and polypropylene, could remain challenging. As you can see from the following chart, the spot prices of these chemicals remain near a 5-year low. Ongoing global demand uncertainties, due to geopolitical uncertainties and tariff policies, could keep the price pressured. Also, in terms of valuation, the stock's current FY1 P/E of 21.3x is certainly not cheap for a cyclical stock with a strong commodity flavor. However, as detailed in my last article, once adjusted for growth and yield, the valuation is quite attractive in terms of PEG (P/E growth ratio) and PEGY (P/E growth yield ratio).

On the positive side, there are also some new developments that could impact near-term supply-demand dynamics and support a more favorable pricing environment. In particular, DOW recently suffered a fire accident in its Freeport, TX plant at the end of 2025. The plant houses several of its large polyethylene production units. My estimate is that the accident could keep around 1.5% of US capacity offline for Q4 2025. You can see that since the end of 2025, polyethylene spot prices have staged a notable rally. Internally, management is working on a Cash Improvement Plan with a focus on reducing costs, improving working capital inflow (which could lead to up to ~$1 billion of benefit in Q4 by my estimate), and also tightening CAPEX. These improvements could help to boost its dividend coverage ratios and/or DCR more rapidly and significantly than the consensus I have projected above.

To close, this reexamination of LYB is largely triggered by two new developments since my last writing: its upcoming FQ4 ER and the latest changes in the spot prices of key petrochemicals. The latest EPS revisions for FQ4 and also full FY2025 do invite concerns over its dividend sustainability. However, investors should be aware of the caveats in EPS coverage ratios. In the case of LYB, I do believe they overstate the coverage pressure and encourage readers to examine other dividend coverage metrics such as cash flow and also DCR.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Buffett Overhang Still Exists
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) has slumped to $40, yet the stock valuation still has an overhang from Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) a major shareholder. The energy sector faces compressed earnings due to lower oil prices while the stock still trades at a sector premium. My investment thesis is more neutral on the stock now due to the relative valuation disconnect offset by the odds of higher energy prices.

Far From $60

Occidental long traded above $60 due to the Buffett put; Berkshire had a long history of loading up on the stock, whether the valuation made any sense. Now, the stock trading $20 lower is no definite indication OXY is more appealing due to the long inflated price.

Berkshire has long made investing decisions based on needing large positions to move the needle with a $1 trillion investment portfolio with over $300 billion in cash now. An individual investor doesn’t have the same restrictions.

The investment firm last bought OXY at nearly $47 and owns 265 million shares worth nearly $13 billion at the time. So even with a massive cash balance, Berkshire still hasn't bought more shares with the stock down, though the firm has preferred stock and warrants currently far out of the money.

Despite the stock dip, JPMorgan analysts outlined a scenario where Occidental still trades at a far lower free cash flow yield compared to peer Devon Energy (DVN). The stock has a free cash flow/enterprise value yield in the 6% range compared to Devon up at 8%.

Occidental has higher leverage as well. The energy company had net debt of ~$21.5 billion at the end of Q3 and should have the number lowered with the OxyChem deal closed on January 2.

Either way, an Occidental investment has the potential for positive outcomes based on higher energy prices. Nat gas just surged 50% in two days due to an impending winter storm in the U.S.

The bigger headwind for the stock is Occidental facing massive earnings compression in 2026. Oil prices are still down to 5-year lows around $60 a barrel from the 2025 starting level of over $75. An equally big issue is selling the OxyChem business to, ironically, Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7 billion in cash.

The company planned to use the proceeds to immediately repay ~$6.5B of debt, with the remaining ~$1.5B of after-tax proceeds going to its balance sheet. Occidental plans to end the period with $15 billion in net debt, but the company has far lower earnings now even with saving $350 million in annual interest expenses.

Notably in these transactions, corporations typically don't point out the lost cash flows and profits in a trade off for obtaining the cash payments. OxyChem was running at about $800 million in annual pre-tax income.

Buffett Multiple

The stock trades at a lofty multiples of 2026/27 EPS targets to go along with the J.P. Morgan cash flow yields. The consensus analyst estimates have 2026 EPS dipping to $1.42 with Occidental now trading at 30x these targets. The stock even trades at 22x the 2027 EPS targets, requiring a 33% boost in earnings.

While the OxyChem deal helps with the balance sheet, the market isn't going to be happy with earnings compression. Occidental was likely far better off just using cash flows to repay debt.

The stock not only faces the tough earnings comparisons through 2026, OXY is just outright expensive compared to peers like Devon discussed above and ConocoPhillips (COP) trading at only 18x forward earnings.

Ultimately, the stock will trade based on energy prices. OXY could actually trade up and provide solid returns to shareholders on a continued jump in nat gas prices or a rebound in oil. Over time, though, OXY will likely underperform the sector due to the ongoing premium valuation overhang.

The company discusses using free cash flows going forward to repurchase shares with remaining debt maturities paid off when due, but the stock really isn't cheap enough to warrant such moves. OXY should just repay debt and save the interest expenses and wait for an actually cheap stock to purchase shares.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Occidental remains aggressively priced. The stock has maintained lofty levels for years now due to the perceived Buffett put and the attractiveness due to Berkshire Hathaway owning the stock, yet blind investments are never an ideal reason to buy a stock, as evidenced by investors buying alongside Berkshire from 2022 to 2024 down ~$20 and still now owning a cheap stock.

data.title: Fuchs SE: The Market Is Mispricing This Business
data.description:
Investment Action

I give a buy rating for Fuchs SE (FUPBY) because the market is under-appreciating the strength of FUPBY’s business; specifically, its pricing power, track record of organic share gains, and a balance sheet that allows it to consolidate weaker competitors.

Company Description

FUPBY is the world’s largest independent lubricant producer. Unlike vertically integrated peers that see lubricants as an add-on, FUPBY makes it its focus, making sure its products perform better and can be customized. FUPBY’s product portfolio has >10,000 specialized formulations across automotive, industrial, metalworking, and corrosion protection. And notably, these are not off-the-shelf lubricants. A meaningful portion of revenue comes from custom-made solutions (co-developed with OEMs and industrial customers). The sales model is decentralized across 42 production sites globally, supported by more than 6,700 employees.

The "Specialty" Moat

The anchor bullish point of my thesis is that FUPBY is the clear number one independent supplier in the global lubricants market. The bar chart above may make FUPBY’s market share look weak, but that comparison is flawed because this is a massive, fragmented market, and what matters is not total share but where that share is. FUPBY's focus is on the specialty end of the spectrum, where volumes are generally lower (since most of them are custom-made to fix end clients) but margins are structurally higher (because of complexity). This in turn also means that FUPBY has structurally higher pricing power.

The reason clients are willing to pay, outside of it being custom-made, is because of the mission critical-ness. It’s about reliability. Think about the operations in a mine or a steel mill; the cost of the lubricant is really irrelevant compared to the cost of downtime. In the event a machine fails to work because of an inferior lubricant, the operator risks shutting down the entire operation. In that context, I would think paying a premium for a proven and reliable product is a rational choice.

One thing that I believe investors may be underappreciating is that FUPBY is not vertically tied to crude production (like other vertically integrated players). Because of that, it isn’t forced to use its own feedstock. It can go out to the market and source the best base oils available and optimize for performance (and cost if need be).

Growth and the EV Misconception

I believe the biggest opportunity here comes from the misconception of internal combustion engine [ICE] vehicles decline hurting FUPBY’s growth. At face value, the bears do seem to have a point. The global lubricants market is flat, with volumes barely growing, but that doesn’t really mean anything in the context of FUPBY since it has grown by ~5% over the past two decades. What this suggests is that FUPBY has been winning share, and hence, the growth story is more of whether FUPBY can continue to win share rather than overall market growth. My take is that FUPBY can continue to win because of its scale advantage. It has the R&D capacity, resources, and balance sheet to meet tighter regulations and deliver on customers’ demand for customized solutions. Whereas for a smaller lubricant player, it really doesn’t have much choice but to cede share since it lacks the resources. And this gap widens as FUPBY scales because of the learning curve (i.e., technical knowledge accumulated over time).

As for the ICE and electric vehicle [EV] debate, I think the bear case is simple. EVs don’t have engines; engines need oil; therefore, lubricants will see lesser demand. That is ultimately true if all vehicles on earth are EVs, but that is not going to be the case anytime soon. In fact, I think it is going to take decades. Using data from Europe and the US, we can see that this transition could go well into the latter half of this century, which means there is still a significant base of ICE vehicles that need to be re-lubricated every now and then. I also point out to the bears that FUPBY is not entirely obsolete even if all vehicles are EVs. The fact is EVs introduce a different problem. While it doesn’t need lubricant, it needs specialized cooling fluid to cool the batteries and e-transmissions, and these are high-spec, high-margin products. FUCHS already has a foothold here with its BluEV portfolio.

If we take a step back, I also note that almost half of FUPBY’s revenue has nothing to do with passenger cars. The other 50% comes from mining, agriculture, aerospace, and heavy industry that still run on mechanical systems that require lubrication. Unlike EVs, I don’t think we are going to replace combine harvesters or mining trucks with batteries anytime soon because of reliability.

Balance Sheet

Given that the growth thesis relies on FUPBY being aggressive in gaining share and scaling up, its balance sheet is something to pay attention to. As of Q3 2025, FUPBY has a cash position of EUR142 million (debt of EUR112 million), which gives it a lot of balance sheet flexibility to pursue M&A to accelerate the pace of scaling up. Remember that the lubricants market is deeply fragmented, with plenty of small regional players that are potential targets for FUPBY. I am not saying this from a theoretical perspective. FUPBY has a long track record of executing bolt-on deals that expand its reach and bring on-board niche capabilities. Some examples include the acquisition of Lubcon, Strub, and Boss Lubricants. These acquisitions tend to be small, but because of FUPBY's scale, when they are integrated, FUPBY is able to extract both revenue and cost synergies through raw materials procurement (global sourcing capability) and distribution scale.

Valuation

The returns algorithm for FUPBY stock is bridged by a few parts. Firstly, earnings should continue to grow at mid-to-high single digits (~5% revenue growth + some margin expansion from scale and M&A). Secondly, FUPBY gives a dividend yield of ~3% to 4%. Based on these two, investors can easily get >10% returns before any change in valuation multiples. This brings me to my last point, multiples. FUPBY trades at ~12x forward PE, a sharp discount vs. Quaker Chemical Corporation (KWR), which trades at ~17x. I don’t get why FUPBY should trade at such a discount since it has similar mid-single-digit growth like KWR, a similar margin, but a much better balance sheet (KWR's leverage ratio is 4.5x). In this comparison, FUPBY should at least trade in line with KWR. If that happens (FUPBY's 10-year historical average is 17x, by the way), say over three years [((17/12) - 1) / 3], the additional returns a year are about 14%. Summing it up, the IRR per year is 5% earnings growth + ~3.5% dividend yield + 14% multiples change = >20% IRR.

Risk

While FUPBY has scale and can likely pass through raw material cost inflation to customers, a sharp spike in base oil or additive prices can cause margins to weaken temporarily due to pass-through lags. One important risk is that FUPBY needs to keep gaining share to grow (since this is a flat market); if it fails to do so, the market could start to price this business at a much lower valuation.

Conclusion

I am recommending a buy rating for FUPBY. I think the market is not fully appreciating the fact that it can continue to win share, is not entirely burdened by the ICE to EV shift narrative, and has a strong balance sheet that gives it the flexibility to invest in growth (either R&D or M&A). At ~12x forward earnings and a 3–4% dividend yield, I think investors can bridge to a decent IRR over the next few years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nathan's Famous: Take The (Kind Of Disappointing) Money And Run
data.description:
In one sense, I called it. Nathan's Famous (NATH) is getting acquired by Smithfield Foods (SFD) in a ~$450 million deal. In a pair of articles last year, I argued that a sale seemed likely and even twice called out Smithfield as the most likely acquirer.

But before dislocating my shoulder trying to pat myself on the back, neither call was all that complicated. The fact that Howard Lorber, NATH's largest shareholder, was 76 and had been pushed out of his role at Douglas Ellman (DOUG) was widely known. So was the licensing deal with Smithfield that underpins the overwhelming majority of Nathan's Famous's current profits. I was far from the only investor to see a deal with Smithfield as logical, and indeed a few rallies over the past couple of years (including the steady accumulation up to ~$115 last summer) looked like the market was positioning for a transaction.

And indeed, neither call was all that spectacular — or that lucrative. With an 8.7% gain on Wednesday as of this writing, total returns in NATH since my first piece are ~23%, and negative 9% since I decided to keep my position last summer.

In that context, I obviously see the $102 per share price announced Wednesday as something of a disappointment. But it's an understandable disappointment, particularly with the business showing a bit of softness over the last couple of quarters. And with the deal spread now in the mid-single digits, disappointing or not, the risk/reward clearly is in favor of cutting loose, as I did on Wednesday afternoon.

Really? $102 a Share?

Again, I can see some disappointment in the price here, which in turn might suggest at least the possibility of a higher bid from over the top. Net debt at the end of Q3 was just $17.1 million; reported diluted shares were 4.13 million, but the actual figure seems to be a touch higher (Smithfield is reporting the deal at "approximately" a $450 million enterprise value, while the math above suggests ~$438 million).

As the press release notes, the deal is also at a 12.4x EBITDA multiple (trailing twelve months through calendar Q3 2025), which certainly seems healthy for a hot dog manufacturer. But the business model here is licensing (mostly to Smithfield itself, of course; the meatpacker's John Morell unit actually manufactures the Nathan's hot dog), which suggests a stronger multiple. That's all the more true given that Smithfield estimates it will save about $9 million a year; adding back those synergies (which get EBITDA to just over $45 million), the EBITDA multiple is just under 10x.

It would seem — and, this summer, it did seem — like a buyer might be interested at a bigger multiple. Smithfield itself paid $33 million in royalties to Nathan's Famous over the past four quarters. Cutting that spend alone seems to justify the purchase price, particularly with other businesses to benefit from. Those businesses are less valuable but at least material: licensing revenue ex-Smithfield over the past four quarters is about $4 million, while the Branded Product Program has brought in $5 million and the restaurant business close to $3 million.

It does seem like $102 is a bit of an underpay. There's an argument that licensing alone supports the $450 million price tag (a 12x multiple for a licensor is hardly out of line and, in fact, represents a discount to admittedly larger and likely better players like Domino's (DPZ)), meaning Nathan's perhaps left some value on the table.

And it would then seem to follow that there might be room for another bid. The termination fee here is $10.6 million, hardly enough to deter a buyer that sees value in Nathan's Famous at $110 per share plus.

A White Knight Seems Unlikely

But another buyer seems unlikely for two core reasons. The first is that Nathan's Famous almost certainly executed a lengthy and thorough sales process. Again, Lorber is a veteran of dealmaking (in both the equity markets and real estate). The Financial Times reported almost a year ago that the company was considering a sale; such a leak would make sense if Nathan's Famous was trying to entice potential suitors.

And so the fact that Smithfield is paying $102/share seems to suggest that was simply the best price Nathan's could get. Again, it's a surprise, but surely both strategic buyers and private equity firms at least looked. PE would make quite a bit of sense here: I've speculated in the past that Nathan's now-underlevered balance sheet was likely designed to potentially attract private equity bids. The board paused buybacks and kept the dividend flat despite earnings growth; neither decision makes sense, particularly because a stable licensing profit stream allowed the company to run higher leverage. (Indeed, Nathan's recapitalized itself back in 2015, briefly running at ~5x EBITDA after paying out a large special dividend.)

The second, likely related, issue is that results the last few quarters weren't great. Adjusted EBITDA declined year-over-year in the first half of fiscal 2026, and volume at retail (through Smithfield) plunged 17%. Price increases are part of the issue (they were up 15%), and the 10-Q cites lower promotional activity as a result. Profits in the BPP were down sharply as well, as the cost per pound for hot dogs jumped 16%, outpacing price increases.

Over the past decade-plus, Nathan's Famous has simply been a good business, steadily growing profits and often returning capital to shareholders. But the last two quarters have shown some potential cracks in the business model and do raise the question of what the brand looks like if costs keep rising in a category where consumers can trade down (or simply not pay $12 per pound for hot dogs, even if they're quality hot dogs). One has to wonder if that sense didn't spook some buyers; it seemed in February like there would be plenty of acquirers, but in the end Nathan's seems to have gone with the best offer from the company most likely to do the deal.

Smithfield and Downside

Assuming beef prices eventually settle, this does look like a good deal for Smithfield. Given the $30 million-plus in royalty payments being saved each year, it almost can't be a terrible deal. And it does advance the company's strategy in building out brands and moving up in price points.

As far as NATH goes, the deal spread is relatively narrow. The merger agreement does allow for the payment of up to more dividends; it's possible the merger closes after the second (which should be scheduled for May; Smithfield expects the deal to close in the first half).

Even considering that, the deal spread here does look quite narrow: 2.2% returns over presumably a five-year holding period. Given that six-month Treasuries are yielding 3.6%, that spread (a bit over 5%) looks pretty tight.

And that spread might suggest that buyers see at least a possibility of a second bidder and/or something close to certainty in the deal being closed. As written above, the former seems unlikely; the latter is probably right — with one notable exception. I've written before, mostly tongue-in-cheek, that it's at least possible the sale here becomes a political football.

The idea of a classic American brand — one founded in the city our current president was born and raised in — being sold to a "Chinese" company (Smithfield is still ~88% owned by Hong Kong-based WH Group) could raise some hackles. And given that President Trump has publicly voiced his opinion on various mergers, most notably in the media space, it wouldn't be stunning if the Nathan's deal became a cause célèbre among the online right. That could eventually catch the president's ear.

To be clear, such an outcome is unlikely. But stranger things have happened in this political environment — and the odds of another bid seem similarly slim. And with the spread so narrow, NATH looks like one to leave for the arbitrage professionals.

data.title: Where And Why For Gold And Silver
data.description:
By Brien Lundin

For those who thought 2025 was crazy for gold and silver, 2026 is saying, “Hold my beer.” So far this year, we’ve seen silver reach for the skies and a target of $100 that seemed like a fantasy, or fanatical, just a few weeks ago.

And during that amazing run, gold has been relatively well-behaved, advancing steadily but in an unspectacular manner, at least in comparison to silver.

That’s changed over the past few trading sessions. As you can see from the chart above, gold has accelerated to the upside, seemingly taking aim for the magical $5,000 level in the near future. (This chart makes it seem like gold is down on Wednesday, but thanks to a surge in overnight markets, it’s actually up over $50 from Tuesday.)

Silver is taking a break on Wednesday, and a much-needed one at that. But if you take all of these moves, including the PGMs, base metals, and just about every other commodity, it’s obvious that something’s up.

Let’s take a look at just what that could be.

Who’s Buying at These Levels?

I ran across a post on X by a gold bear this morning, and his point was essentially, “Who do you know who’s actually buying gold at these prices?” Well, if your worldview is limited to U.S. retail investors, then everyone you know is more likely to sell gold than buy right now.

But it’s a great big world, and there are plenty of actors on the global stage who think that gold and silver are bargains at these prices. Of course, we all know about central banks.

They’re not very price sensitive, and apparently are steadily buying on a dollar-cost averaging basis at regular intervals. This means that individual banks are probably buying less in terms of tonnage as the price rises.

The good news for gold bulls is that others are stepping in to pick up whatever slack there is in official purchases. These include investors and institutions in China and India (especially for silver, where the local prices are well above the New York quotes).

And everywhere else around the world, it seems that the “sell America” trade is back on in force, especially given the noise around Greenland. Add in the surge in Japan’s sovereign bond yields, which threatens not only other sovereign yields but every risk market, and you have a powerful recipe for continued gains in monetary metals.

The other metals and commodities are benefiting from strong demand curves running smack-dab into decades of under-investment and strangled supply pipelines. With commodities becoming the latest theme du jour on Wall Street, this trend also seems set to continue. And in turn, this raises the question...

How Much Further Can We Go?

I’ve addressed this question in every presentation I’ve given over the past five years or so, and in those speeches as well as this letter, I’ve featured this slide:

I’ve counted four distinct bull markets since August 15, 1971, when gold began trading as an investable asset, and not the basis of the global financial system. Those four bull markets occurred in the early 1970s, the late 1970s, the 2000s, and today.

In the chart above, I show that gold rose from 5.6 times to 8.2 times, from trough to peak, in each of those previous bull markets. If you count the bottom of this market at around $1,050, then a similar performance this time would put the gold price somewhere between $6,000 and $8,000.

But there’s another possible interpretation...

And that would be if we consider the 1970s as one cohesive bull run, as many analysts do. If that’s the case, then gold rose some 24 times during that period, which would project to a price in the mid-$20,000s at the end of this bull market.

That, in turn, would imply some sort of monetary reset, with gold re-inserted as the foundation for fiat currencies globally. And that’s certainly not out of the question.

It would also imply an interim break in the bull market, and a fairly severe one, like what was experienced in the 1974-1976 timeframe. That’s a possibility that we need to be ready for this time around.

The bottom line is that no one really knows what the future holds for gold, silver, and the rest of the commodity complex. But one thing is clear, and that’s the direction of the trend.

It’s also clear that we want to be on board with this trend, because it’s going a lot higher, albeit with some volatility along the way.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial Partners: Attractive With The Synovus Deal Completed
data.description:
Shares of Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Investors suffered whiplash this summer as Pinnacle agreed to buy Synovus after many expected Pinnacle itself to get acquired. The deal was completed on Jan. 2 and is a true “merger of equals” with PNFP holders owning just 52% of the combined company. While there is execution risk, I am bullish on this combination, which drove my “Buy” rating in October, and since then, shares have gained 12%. With updated financials and the merger complete, now is a good time to revisit PNFP.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Pinnacle Financial Partners earned $2.24 per share, which was $0.02 below expectations even as revenue grew 14% to $542 million. Still, earnings were up 18% from last year, reflecting strong balance sheet growth. There was a lot to this quarter since PNFP closed its SNV purchase right after quarter end, so no SNV financials are consolidated into its results but were instead released separately. Including Synovus, Pinnacle has about $117 billion of assets and $96 billion in deposits across its 400 locations in the South.

PNFP reports strong growth; credit costs unlikely to remain elevated

Looking first at legacy PNFP, loans rose 10% from last year to $39.2 billion, and deposits were up 11% to $47.4 billion. As you can see below, PNFP has steadily grown its loan book, and yields are now coming down given Fed rate cuts. Importantly, non-interest-bearing deposits have kept pace, also rising 11% to $9 billion, and it appears these have bottomed as a share of deposits, though I am not expecting any recovery in 2026. Deposit costs ended the quarter down 22 bp to 2.21%, and PNFP has been able to cut rates as quickly as it raised them during the hiking cycle while maintaining strong deposit growth.

PNFP generated $424 million of net interest income, up 13% from last year, and its net interest margin of 3.27% was up 1 bp sequentially. Over the next year, PNFP has over $3.5 billion in loans maturing at a ~5% fixed rate. With current loan rates at 7+%, the company can replace these with higher yields and further boost NIM and interest income.

On credit quality, charge-offs were a bit higher than expected at 28 bps, up 4 bps from last year. However, we also saw nonperforming loans drop to 0.34% of loans, down 5 bps sequentially and 8 bps from last year. During the quarter, Pinnacle set aside $34 million for credit losses. Frankly, it seemed PNFP used Q4 to “clear the deck” and pull forward some charge-offs to provide a clean slate for 2026 with its Synovus merger. Over time, NPAs should drive charge-offs and the steady improvement over the past year should ensure charge-offs run closer to 20bps than 30bps going forward. While higher credit costs drove the miss, I do not expect that to be a recurring theme going forward.

Pinnacle remains well capitalized with a tier 1 common equity (CET1) ratio of 10.9%, up from 10.8% a year ago. The ability to build capital while growing loans in double digits is impressive and speaks to the strong earnings power of the franchise. This leaves Pinnacle with ample financial capacity to support ongoing loan growth, and with the company continuing to aggressively hire revenue-producing bankers (200+ people this year), that remains the clear priority of the firm.

Synovus results were as expected

Now, while Synovus is no longer publicly traded, Pinnacle released its Q4 results as well to give investors a sense of how that business was doing before being acquired. It earned $1.45, which was similar to its Q2 and Q3 results. Importantly, balance sheet momentum continued with loans up 2% sequentially and deposits growing 3% sequentially to $51.2 billion. Given fixed reinvestment and a 16bp drop in deposit costs to 2.07%, SNV’s net interest margin expanded 4bps to 3.45%.

Credit quality also remains solid at legacy Synovus. Net charge-offs were 22 bps, down 4 bps from a year ago, and nonperforming assets were 0.62%, down 11 bps from a year ago. As you can see below, its criticized loans have been steadily falling. Synovus also added $9 million to its credit reserves, which now total $529 million, reflecting ongoing loan growth. At ~5.5x annual charge-offs, SNV is well reserved and already prepared for a modest deterioration in credit quality. The bank remained extremely well capitalized with an 11.3% CET1 ratio; this capital calculation excludes the $628 million loss in accumulated other comprehensive income. Essentially, Q4 transpired as one would expect at Synovus ahead of its acquisition.

2026 outlook is positive for the new PNFP

Now, alongside results, Pinnacle provided 2026 guidance that includes the contribution from Synovus. Pinnacle expects to maintain its strong pace of loan growth with the combined firm growing about 10%. Given PNFP achieved this in 2025, I believe it can do so again, as the loan demand environment should be better this year with less macro uncertainty and lower borrowing costs. Management also expects a broadly similar pace of net charge-offs, consistent with my view that the credit environment should be flattish this year, given slow but positive economic growth.

In terms of integration items related to SNV, management still expects $720 million of merger-related costs through 2027. It continues to target $250 million of cost synergies, though it expects to achieve just 40% of them this year, from 50% previously. Now, when a bank buys another, it marks the assets to market, from an accounting perspective, realizing the loss in AOCI noted above as well as marking loans to the current rate environment. Given changes in rates, it expects -$1.6 billion of marks from -$1.8 billion previously. Because of this, its pro forma capital will be better than first thought. Taken together, I view these developments as offsetting and largely neutral.

The below is a helpful walk of the capital impact of the deal. While the combined entities have an 11.1% CET1 ratio, marking the SNV assets to market immediately moves it to 10%. From there, earnings should offset integration costs, the dividend, and loan growth. Across my coverage of the regional banks over the past few years, I have emphasized that banks' reported CET1 is different from their “pro forma” CET1, including unrealized losses, and investors need to consider both metrics. Essentially, by acquiring SNV, PNFP is “realizing” much of that gap in its regulatory capital position by marking assets to their Day 1 fair value. This makes its regulatory capital ratio more consistent with its true economic position. While the combined regulatory capital ratio is lower, PNFP/SNV’s true financial position is no different. I view ~10% as an appropriate capital level for the combined bank. PNFP plans a $0.50 dividend going forward, for a ~2% dividend yield. Aside from this dividend, I expect PNFP to retain capital to support loan growth and do not view buybacks as likely.

PNFP is a "Buy"

Back in October, I saw PNFP earning $8.75-$9.25 in 2026 on a standalone basis and $9.50-$10 with Synovus, depending on the pace of synergies. We are getting essentially a 12-month contribution from SNV, whereas I previously assumed 10 months, but this benefit is partially offset by a slower realization of synergies. On net, I see Pinnacle earning $9.90-$10.50 this year, excluding one-time merger costs. This leaves shares right around 10x earnings, which I view as attractive for a firm with solid credit quality, clear loan growth momentum, and a good economic capital position. Plus, as synergies are fully realized, that provides a runway for ongoing earnings growth in 2027 past $11, even as I do not assume any of the $130 million in targeted revenue synergies.

Q1 results will likely be very messy on a GAAP basis, given the mark-to-market and subsequent reallocation of legacy SNV’s securities portfolio, but I believe we will quickly see underlying core results show momentum as Pinnacle capitalizes on its leading presence across the Southeast. I expect shares to migrate towards 12x over the next 12-18 months, providing eventual upside towards $130. As such, I remain a "Buy" on PNFP and would accumulate shares here.
